chemicals.name: Rotenone
chemicals.description: A natural pesticide found in certain plants. Blocks electron transfer from the iron-sulfur clusters of Complex I to ubiquinone (CoQ). Prevents NADH oxidation and proton pumping at Complex I.
chemicals.overview: Natural plant-derived insecticide and pesticide. Shuts down the first step of the electron transport chain, preventing cells from using NADH to generate ATP.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: EPA: 'Not Likely to be Carcinogenic to Humans.' No IARC classification. However, linked to Parkinson's disease risk through mitochondrial damage in dopaminergic neurons.
chemicals.common_source: Natural plant insecticide from derris root and jicama vines; used as a fish poison in South America and parts of Asia.

chemicals.name: Piericidin A
chemicals.description: A potent antibiotic produced by Streptomyces. Structurally similar to ubiquinone, it competes for the CoQ binding site on Complex I, blocking electron flow.
chemicals.overview: Bacterial antibiotic that mimics ubiquinone's shape. Tricks Complex I by fitting into the CoQ docking site, blocking electron handoff to the mobile carrier.
chemicals.common_source: Bacterial antibiotic produced by Streptomyces mobaraensis and related species; used only as a research tool to probe Complex I.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC, NTP, or EPA. No established carcinogenic risk; used only in research and not an environmental exposure.

chemicals.name: Amobarbital (Barbiturate)
chemicals.description: Barbiturates like amobarbital inhibit Complex I by blocking electron transfer at the NADH dehydrogenase. Reduces proton pumping and NADH-linked respiration.
chemicals.overview: A sedative/anesthetic drug. At high doses it inhibits Complex I, contributing to respiratory depression and cellular energy failure in overdose.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: IARC Group 2B (Phenobarbital, related compound): Possibly carcinogenic to humans. Barbiturates as a class associated with increased liver tumor risk in animal studies.
chemicals.common_source: Pharmaceutical sedative/anesthetic; historically prescribed for insomnia, anxiety, and seizures (Amytal, Nembutal, Phenobarbital).

chemicals.name: MPP⁺ (from MPTP)
chemicals.description: MPTP is converted to MPP⁺ by MAO-B in the brain. MPP⁺ is selectively taken up by dopaminergic neurons and inhibits Complex I, causing energy depletion and oxidative stress.
chemicals.overview: A synthetic contaminant discovered in tainted street drugs. Converted to MPP⁺ in the brain, where it selectively kills dopamine neurons by poisoning their Complex I — causing rapid-onset Parkinson's disease.
chemicals.common_source: Synthetic contaminant discovered in tainted 'synthetic heroin' (MPPP) in the 1980s; now used only in research to model Parkinson's disease in animals.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC. MPTP/MPP+ toxicity is acute dopaminergic neurotoxicity causing Parkinson's-like syndrome, not carcinogenicity.

chemicals.name: Metformin
chemicals.description: First-line diabetes drug that mildly inhibits Complex I. Reduces ATP production, activating AMPK which increases glucose uptake and reduces hepatic glucose output. Effect is much weaker than rotenone.
chemicals.overview: The world's most prescribed diabetes medication. Gently slows Complex I just enough to trigger the cell's energy sensor (AMPK), which boosts glucose uptake — a therapeutic side effect of mild ETC inhibition.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not carcinogenic. Epidemiological studies consistently show metformin is associated with REDUCED cancer risk. Actively studied as a potential anti-cancer agent due to AMPK activation and metabolic effects on tumors.
chemicals.common_source: Pharmaceutical first-line diabetes drug; originally derived from guanidine compounds found in Galega officinalis (French lilac / goat's rue).

chemicals.name: Phenformin
chemicals.description: A biguanide drug that inhibits Complex I more potently than metformin. Withdrawn from most markets in the 1970s due to unacceptably high rates of fatal lactic acidosis caused by excessive suppression of oxidative phosphorylation.
chemicals.overview: Metformin's dangerous older sibling. Both inhibit Complex I, but phenformin hits it much harder — so hard that lactate builds up fatally. Withdrawn worldwide after causing hundreds of deaths, replaced by the gentler metformin.
chemicals.common_source: Synthetic biguanide drug; introduced in the 1950s for diabetes, withdrawn from the US (1978) and most countries due to fatal lactic acidosis. Still available in a few countries.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic. Like metformin, some evidence suggests anti-cancer properties via AMPK activation, but clinical use was terminated before long-term cancer data could be collected.

chemicals.name: Malonate
chemicals.description: A competitive inhibitor of succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II). Structurally similar to succinate, it occupies the active site and prevents succinate from being oxidized. Reversible — can be outcompeted by excess succinate.
chemicals.overview: A simple organic acid that mimics succinate closely enough to jam Complex II's active site. Used in labs to study the FADH2 branch of the ETC. Unlike TTFA (which blocks the CoQ exit), malonate blocks the succinate entry point.
chemicals.common_source: Naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid found in many fruits (especially grapes and apples); used as a standard competitive inhibitor in biochemistry research.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified. A natural metabolite with no documented carcinogenicity.

chemicals.name: TTFA (Thenoyltrifluoroacetone)
chemicals.description: Inhibits Complex II by blocking electron transfer from FADH2 to ubiquinone at the CoQ binding site. Prevents succinate-driven respiration.
chemicals.overview: A research chemical that blocks the exit point of Complex II where electrons are handed off to CoQ. Used in labs to study the FADH2 branch of the ETC independently.
chemicals.common_source: Synthetic research chemical, originally developed as a metal chelator, now used in the lab to probe the ubiquinone binding site of Complex II.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified. Research-only chemical with no documented carcinogenicity data.

chemicals.name: 3-Nitropropionic Acid (3-NPA)
chemicals.description: An irreversible inhibitor of succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II). Covalently modifies the enzyme's active site, permanently inactivating it. Causes selective striatal neurodegeneration resembling Huntington's disease.
chemicals.overview: A mold toxin that permanently destroys Complex II. Found in moldy sugarcane that has caused mass poisonings in China. Selectively damages the striatum in the brain — used in research to model Huntington's disease.
chemicals.common_source: Produced by the fungus Arthrinium saccharicola on stored sugarcane; responsible for outbreaks of acute encephalopathy in China. Also found in some legumes (Astragalus species).
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC. Primarily an acute neurotoxin; no established carcinogenicity.

chemicals.name: Antimycin A
chemicals.description: Binds the Qi site (the quinone-reduction pocket on the matrix side of Complex III / cytochrome bc1), blocking the second half of the Q-cycle so electrons cannot be recycled onto a new ubiquinone. Halts electron transfer from ubiquinol to cytochrome c and stops proton pumping at Complex III.
chemicals.overview: A fish poison and antibiotic. Jams Complex III's internal recycling mechanism (Q-cycle), causing electrons to leak onto oxygen and generate toxic free radicals.
chemicals.common_source: Produced by Streptomyces kitazawaensis; historically used as a piscicide (fish poison) in fisheries management and now a standard research reagent.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC. Used as a piscicide with environmental safeguards; no established human carcinogenicity.

chemicals.name: Myxothiazol
chemicals.description: Binds the Qo site (the ubiquinol-oxidation pocket on the IMS side of Complex III), preventing ubiquinol from handing electrons to the Rieske iron-sulfur cluster. Unlike Antimycin A (which blocks the Qi site), myxothiazol blocks the FIRST step of the Q-cycle.
chemicals.overview: A bacterial antibiotic that blocks the entry point of Complex III where reduced CoQ delivers its electrons. Stops the chain at the midpoint.
chemicals.common_source: Bacterial antibiotic from Myxococcus fulvus; used exclusively in research to probe the Q-cycle of Complex III.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified. Research chemical with no documented human carcinogenicity data.

chemicals.name: Stigmatellin
chemicals.description: Binds the Qo site (the quinol-oxidation pocket on the IMS side of Complex III), preventing ubiquinol from donating electrons. Unlike Antimycin A (Qi site), Qo-site inhibitors generate less ROS because they prevent semiquinone formation rather than trapping it.
chemicals.overview: A research antibiotic that blocks Complex III at a different site than Antimycin A. By blocking electron entry (Qo) rather than exit (Qi), it produces far fewer toxic free radicals — an important distinction for understanding ROS biology.
chemicals.common_source: Produced by the myxobacterium Stigmatella aurantiaca; used as a research tool to distinguish Qo vs Qi site mechanisms in Complex III.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified. Research-only compound with no documented carcinogenicity.

chemicals.name: Atovaquone
chemicals.description: A hydroxynaphthoquinone that inhibits the cytochrome bc1 complex (Complex III) at the Qo site. Highly selective for parasite mitochondria over human mitochondria, making it useful as an antimalarial and anti-Pneumocystis drug.
chemicals.overview: An FDA-approved drug used to treat malaria and Pneumocystis pneumonia. It exploits a key difference: parasite Complex III is much more sensitive to Qo-site inhibition than human Complex III, so therapeutic doses kill parasites while sparing host cells.
chemicals.common_source: Synthetic pharmaceutical (brand: Mepron, Malarone). FDA-approved for treatment and prevention of malaria (with proguanil) and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia in immunocompromised patients.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic. FDA-approved drug with extensive safety data; no carcinogenicity signals in clinical use.

chemicals.name: Cyanide (CN⁻)
chemicals.description: Binds to the Fe³⁺ in cytochrome a3 of Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase), preventing the final transfer of electrons to oxygen. Completely halts the entire ETC.
chemicals.overview: One of the most rapidly lethal poisons. Locks onto the iron in Complex IV so electrons cannot reach oxygen. The entire chain backs up and ATP production stops within seconds.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic by IARC, NTP, or EPA. Cyanide is an acute systemic toxin, not a carcinogen — it kills cells by energy deprivation, not DNA damage.
chemicals.common_source: Present in almond, cherry, apricot, and apple seeds; cassava; industrial metal-extraction and electroplating; historically used as a chemical weapon.

chemicals.name: Carbon Monoxide (CO)
chemicals.description: Binds to the reduced iron (Fe²⁺) of cytochrome a3 in Complex IV, competing with oxygen. Prevents electron transfer to O2 and blocks the entire chain.
chemicals.overview: A colorless, odorless gas from incomplete combustion. Poisons cells in two ways: binds hemoglobin (blocking oxygen delivery) AND directly blocks Complex IV (blocking oxygen use).
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic by IARC. CO is an acute asphyxiant, not a carcinogen. However, combustion sources that produce CO often co-emit carcinogens (e.g., benzene, PAHs).
chemicals.common_source: Produced by incomplete combustion: car exhaust, house fires, gas appliances, tobacco smoke, and charcoal grills in enclosed spaces.

chemicals.name: Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)
chemicals.description: Inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) by binding to the copper center (CuB). At low concentrations may act as a signaling molecule; at high concentrations it is toxic.
chemicals.overview: The 'rotten egg' gas. At low levels it is a natural signaling molecule in the body. At high levels it poisons Complex IV similarly to cyanide, shutting down aerobic respiration.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic by IARC or NTP. Primarily an acute toxic gas. Some research suggests low-level H₂S may actually have anti-cancer properties through mitochondrial signaling.
chemicals.common_source: Rotting organic matter, sewers, volcanic gases, oil and gas wells, paper mills, and deep natural-gas deposits.

chemicals.name: Sodium Azide (NaN₃)
chemicals.description: Azide ions bind to cytochrome a3 in Complex IV, similar to cyanide. Blocks electron flow to oxygen and halts oxidative phosphorylation.
chemicals.overview: A chemical preservative and airbag propellant. Highly toxic if ingested — acts like cyanide by blocking Complex IV. Prevents cells from using oxygen to make ATP.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: NTP: Known mutagen (positive in Ames test and other genotoxicity assays). Not formally classified as carcinogenic by IARC, but mutagenic potential warrants caution.
chemicals.common_source: Automobile airbag propellant, lab reagent preservative in biochemistry buffers, and some commercial herbicides.

chemicals.name: Oligomycin
chemicals.description: Blocks the proton channel (Fo subunit) of ATP synthase (Complex V). Protons cannot flow back into the matrix, so ATP synthesis stops. The proton gradient builds up, eventually slowing the entire ETC.
chemicals.overview: A fungal antibiotic that plugs the proton channel in ATP synthase. Protons pile up in the IMS with nowhere to go, creating backpressure that eventually stalls the whole chain.
chemicals.common_source: Produced by Streptomyces diastatochromogenes; used exclusively as a research tool to block ATP synthase.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC. Used only in research settings; not a documented human carcinogen.

chemicals.name: 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP)
chemicals.description: A protonophore that carries H⁺ across the inner mitochondrial membrane, bypassing ATP synthase. Dissipates the proton gradient as heat. Electron transport accelerates but ATP production drops dramatically.
chemicals.overview: A dangerous weight-loss drug banned in the 1930s. Pokes holes in the proton gradient — the energy meant for ATP is wasted as heat instead, raising body temperature dangerously.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC. NTP: Limited evidence. DNP is primarily an acute metabolic poison (hyperthermia), but its nitroaromatic structure raises concern for potential mutagenicity.
chemicals.common_source: Synthetic chemical historically sold as a weight-loss drug in the 1930s (now banned); still appears illegally in supplements. Also used in explosives and dye manufacturing.

chemicals.name: FCCP
chemicals.description: Carbonyl cyanide-p-trifluoromethoxyphenylhydrazone. A potent protonophore that shuttles H⁺ across the membrane. Used to measure maximal respiratory capacity in experiments.
chemicals.overview: A powerful research uncoupler. Carries protons across the membrane like a shuttle bus, completely bypassing ATP synthase. Used to measure how fast the ETC can run without any brakes.
chemicals.common_source: Synthetic research chemical; the standard reagent for measuring maximal mitochondrial respiratory capacity in cell biology experiments.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified. Research chemical used in controlled lab settings; no carcinogenicity data.

chemicals.name: CCCP
chemicals.description: Carbonyl cyanide m-chlorophenylhydrazone. Similar to FCCP, it dissipates the proton gradient by shuttling protons across the inner membrane independently of ATP synthase.
chemicals.overview: Another research protonophore, functionally identical to FCCP. Collapses the proton gradient so the ETC runs freely but makes no ATP — all energy is released as heat.
chemicals.common_source: Synthetic research chemical; a standard lab protonophore, functionally interchangeable with FCCP in most experiments.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified. Similar profile to FCCP; research-only reagent with no documented carcinogenicity.

chemicals.name: Atractyloside
chemicals.description: Inhibits the adenine nucleotide translocase (ANT), which normally exchanges ADP (into matrix) for ATP (out of matrix). Blocks ATP export from mitochondria, causing intramitochondrial ATP accumulation and feedback inhibition.
chemicals.overview: A plant toxin from thistles. Blocks the transporter that ships ATP out of the mitochondria. ATP gets trapped inside while the rest of the cell starves for energy.
chemicals.common_source: Plant toxin from the Mediterranean thistle Atractylis gummifera and related species; responsible for livestock and human poisonings.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC. Primarily an acute toxin causing liver and kidney failure, not a long-term cancer risk.
